prompt: Editorial CMS with admin panel, public website, media library, preview, and scheduled publishing for articles

decisions: CMS content is modeled in explicit collections/globals with stable slugs and typed relationships.
Media storage starts on local filesystem with a clear adapter boundary for a later object-storage swap.
Public assets should be delivered through a CDN.
Use SSR or ISR for SEO-sensitive public pages when applicable.
Use SSR or ISR for SEO-sensitive pages.
Serve static assets through CDN.
Implement structured logging.
Add health check endpoints.
Use connection pooling.
Add automated database backups.
Introduce background worker for scheduled tasks.
Store media in S3-compatible object storage.
Media assets stored in object storage.
Background worker processes scheduled jobs.
Authentication handled via secure session or JWT.
Authorization must enforce role and permission boundaries.
Public-facing pages should use caching and delivery strategies appropriate for anonymous traffic.
SEO-sensitive public pages should use rendering strategies such as SSR or ISR when beneficial.
Introduce caching for frequently accessed public content.
CDN recommended for public assets.
Add monitoring and logging stack.

communication.pattern: REST via Payload auto-generated API + custom endpoints
communication.auth: JWT bearer token or session cookie (Payload built-in)

communication.pattern: Drizzle ORM (Payload built-in) over postgres connection pool
S3-compatible API for media upload and retrieval
Direct postgres connection for job execution
Edge cache proxying static assets and SSR/ISR responses

backend: Content model definition via Payload collections
Media asset management and storage orchestration

stories.title: Define CMS content model
stories.description: Define the CMS content schema, globals, and editorial relationships.
acceptance_criteria: The CMS schema explicitly defines at least one primary content collection with slug-based lookup and one supporting media collection.
Only the collections/globals listed in this story are created; no extra editorial schema is invented outside the spec.
Content editors can create and edit entries for each modeled collection/global through the admin surface.
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
All collections and globals are registered in `src/payload.config.ts` using dedicated definition files.
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement draft/publish transitions, preview mode, or scheduled publishing in this story.
Do NOT build media library UI or public page routes here — only define the schema and registration points.
Do NOT add collections/globals beyond the ones named in this story unless `spec.json` is updated first.

validation.manual_check: Admin can create sample page/post entries, assign author/media relations, and edit globals without missing fields.

stories.title: Configure static asset delivery
stories.description: Configure Next.js static asset optimization, cache headers, and Image component for public site delivery.
acceptance_criteria: next.config.ts configures image domains/remotePatterns for external images if applicable
Static assets (JS, CSS, fonts) have appropriate Cache-Control headers via Next.js output config
A shared public image rendering path uses Next.js Image (via a new PublicImage component or an updated existing public component)
The asset/header configuration is isolated to public delivery concerns and does not require creating public routes in this story
Admin routes remain unaffected by caching configuration
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement an external CDN provider (Cloudflare, CloudFront, etc.) — use Next.js built-in optimization only
Do NOT implement the full public site layout — only the delivery infrastructure
Do NOT implement SEO meta tags in this story
expected_files: next.config.ts (updated with images and headers config)
src/components/PublicImage.tsx or equivalent shared public image component

validation.manual_check: Static assets have cache headers; Next.js Image component renders optimized images

stories.title: Initialize project repository (part 1 of 2)
stories.description: Create project structure using nextjs + payload + postgres.
acceptance_criteria: package.json exists with scripts: dev, build, lint, test, typecheck
.env.example exists with all required environment variables
.env.local is already generated by the scaffold with working defaults — do NOT overwrite it
TypeScript is configured with tsconfig.json
ESLint and Prettier are configured
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement any features yet — this is project scaffolding only
Do NOT add authentication, roles, or any business logic
Do NOT create database tables — that is a separate story
Do NOT create files in src/pages/ — this project uses the App Router (src/app/) exclusively
Use environment variable names exactly as defined in .env.example — do NOT rename or create alternatives

validation.manual_check: Project directory exists with expected structure; npm test runs a real test suite

stories.title: Initialize project repository (part 2 of 2)
stories.description: Create project structure using nextjs + payload + postgres.
acceptance_criteria: Vitest is installed and configured so that `npm test` runs a real test runner, not a no-op
At least one smoke test exists (e.g. a test that imports the app config and asserts it loads)
npm install completes without errors
npm run build passes
npm test passes (must not be a no-op)
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement any features yet — this is project scaffolding only
Do NOT add authentication, roles, or any business logic
Do NOT create database tables — that is a separate story
Do NOT create files in src/pages/ — this project uses the App Router (src/app/) exclusively
Use environment variable names exactly as defined in .env.example — do NOT rename or create alternatives

validation.manual_check: Project directory exists with expected structure; npm test runs a real test suite

stories.title: Setup database
stories.description: Configure postgres database with connection pool and migrations.
acceptance_criteria: postgres connection is configured via environment variable
Database client or ORM is installed and configured
Initial migration or schema setup runs without errors
Connection can be verified programmatically
docker-compose.yml includes database service
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
scope_boundaries: Do NOT create application tables yet — only the connection layer
Do NOT add seed data
Do NOT implement any API endpoints
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)

validation.manual_check: Database container starts and connection succeeds

stories.title: Setup backend service
stories.description: Initialize backend using payload with database connection.
acceptance_criteria: Backend is initialized using payload
Server starts and responds to health check
API is accessible at the configured port
Payload config file exists with database adapter configured
Payload admin panel is accessible at /admin
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement authentication yet
Do NOT create domain collections or models yet
Do NOT add business logic — only the backend skeleton
Use environment variable names exactly as defined in .env.example — do NOT rename or create alternatives

stories.title: Create frontend application
stories.description: Implement frontend application shell using nextjs.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend application renders using nextjs
Root page loads without errors
Basic layout component exists (shell, navigation placeholder)
Next.js app router is configured with route groups
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement pages beyond the root layout and a placeholder home page
Do NOT add authentication UI — that is a separate story
Do NOT style extensively — basic layout only
Do NOT create files in src/pages/ — this project uses the App Router (src/app/) exclusively
Use environment variable names exactly as defined in .env.example — do NOT rename or create alternatives

validation.manual_check: Frontend loads in browser without errors

stories.title: Implement authentication
stories.description: Add registration, login, logout and session management.
acceptance_criteria: Users can register with email and password — POST /api/auth/register returns 201 on success, 400 on validation error, 409 on duplicate email
Users can log in and receive a valid session — POST /api/auth/login returns 200 with session token on success, 401 on invalid credentials
Users can log out and have their session invalidated — POST /api/auth/logout returns 200 on success
Protected routes return 401 for unauthenticated requests
Passwords are hashed before storage
Payload Users collection has auth enabled
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement OAuth or social login in this story
Do NOT implement password reset or email verification
Do NOT implement MFA
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)

validation.manual_check: Can register, log in, and access protected route

stories.title: Implement role-based access control
stories.description: Define roles (admin, editor, reviewer), permissions, and enforce authorization boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Role model is defined with the following roles: admin, editor, reviewer
`editor` can create and update drafts but cannot publish, manage users, or change system configuration
`reviewer` can review submissions and publish approved content but cannot manage users or global system configuration
`admin` can manage users, configuration, and override editorial publish/archive actions
Protected endpoints and admin collection/global access rules enforce the role matrix consistently
Unauthorized role access returns 403
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
Payload access control functions explicitly gate editorial collections/globals by admin/editor/reviewer responsibilities
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement granular per-field permissions unless required by spec
Do NOT implement a permissions admin UI — enforce in backend only
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)

validation.manual_check: Editor can edit drafts but gets 403 on publish-only actions; reviewer can approve/publish; admin can manage users.

stories.title: Implement media library
stories.description: Allow uploading, listing, and managing media assets.
acceptance_criteria: Users can upload images and files
Uploaded media is stored and retrievable via URL
Media list view shows uploaded assets with thumbnails
File size and type validation is enforced on upload
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
Payload Media collection uses the local filesystem upload adapter (store uploads in a `media/` directory at the project root)
The local filesystem path is treated as an adapter boundary so object storage can be introduced later without changing callers
Payload Media collection is configured with upload settings
scope_boundaries: Do NOT redefine the CMS schema here — extend the existing `media` collection from the CMS content model story with upload/storage behavior only
Do NOT implement image optimization or CDN delivery in this story
Do NOT implement media usage tracking across content
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)
Do NOT configure S3 or external object storage — use Payload's local disk adapter for now
expected_files: src/payload.config.ts (updated with upload and storage settings)

validation.manual_check: Can upload an image or document, retrieve its URL, and see validation reject disallowed file types.

stories.title: Implement draft and publish workflow
stories.description: Add draft, review, and publish states with role-based transitions.
acceptance_criteria: Collections intended for public rendering (posts, pages) support editorial states `draft`, `in_review`, and `published`
Editors can save drafts and reviewers/admins can transition content to published state (admin or reviewer)
A draft can be sent back from review for changes without creating duplicate entries
The query/filter layer used by future public rendering excludes draft entries unless preview/draft mode is explicitly active
Publishing records a canonical published timestamp or Payload published-state equivalent used by later public rendering
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
Publishing requires admin or reviewer role
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement versioning or revision history
Do NOT implement scheduled publishing — that is a separate story
Do NOT implement approval notifications
Do NOT implement public page components in this story — only the workflow and publish visibility rules
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)

validation.manual_check: Can create a draft, submit/review it, publish it, and confirm only published entries are returned by the public-facing query/filter path.

stories.title: Implement content preview
stories.description: Allow previewing unpublished content in the frontend.
acceptance_criteria: A preview entry route/handler enables draft mode only for authenticated editorial roles
Preview accepts collection + slug lookup for posts, pages and resolves draft content with the same loaders used by public pages
Preview mode can be exited explicitly and public routes immediately return to published-only behavior
Preview responses are marked no-store/noindex and do not leak draft content to anonymous users or search engines
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement live editing or inline editing
Do NOT create a separate second public site just for preview
Do NOT allow anonymous preview links in this story

validation.manual_check: While logged in as an editorial role, enable preview for a draft page/post, verify it renders, then exit preview and confirm the public slug still hides the draft.

stories.title: Implement scheduled publishing (part 1 of 2)
stories.description: Install node-cron, create a background job that publishes content at the scheduled time.
acceptance_criteria: node-cron (or equivalent) is installed as a dependency
A cron job is registered from application startup and runs on a configurable interval (default: every minute)
Content items can have a publishAt date/time field
Collections posts, pages expose scheduling fields/state used by the job without changing slug-based public lookup
The cron job queries for content where publishAt <= now AND status = 'scheduled', and transitions it to 'published'
Each publish attempt is logged with timestamp, content ID, and outcome
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement a full job queue system (Bull, BullMQ) — node-cron is sufficient
Do NOT implement scheduled unpublishing in this story
Do NOT implement a job dashboard or management UI
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)
expected_files: .env.example (updated with SCHEDULED_PUBLISH_CRON or equivalent scheduler interval)

validation.manual_check: Set a draft page/post to `scheduled` with a near-future publishAt, wait for one cron cycle, and verify the public route starts serving it automatically.

stories.title: Implement scheduled publishing (part 2 of 2)
stories.description: Install node-cron, create a background job that publishes content at the scheduled time.
acceptance_criteria: Failed publish attempts are logged and retried on the next cron cycle
The job is idempotent — running it twice does not double-publish
Database migration is generated and executed for all schema changes (run `npm run db:migrate:create` then `npm run db:migrate`)
Successful scheduled publishes make content visible to the existing public rendering path without requiring a manual republish
Publish success triggers the existing public-rendering refresh/revalidation path or logs that it must happen immediately
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement a full job queue system (Bull, BullMQ) — node-cron is sufficient
Do NOT implement scheduled unpublishing in this story
Do NOT implement a job dashboard or management UI
All migrations MUST be created in `src/lib/migrations/` using `npm run db:migrate:create` — do NOT create migration files in any other directory (e.g. `src/models/migrations/`)
expected_files: src/server.ts or payload startup entry (updated to register scheduler)

validation.manual_check: Set a draft page/post to `scheduled` with a near-future publishAt, wait for one cron cycle, and verify the public route starts serving it automatically.

stories.title: Implement public site pages
stories.description: Create public-facing pages for published content: posts, pages.
acceptance_criteria: Public route `/posts/[slug]` exists and renders published content for the `posts` collection
Public route `/pages/[slug]` exists and renders published content for the `pages` collection
Each public route loads content by unique slug from the matching CMS collection instead of hardcoded page data
Public pages use SSR or ISR for SEO (meta tags, Open Graph)
Only published content is visible on public routes — draft content returns 404
Public pages are accessible without authentication
Published-only filtering is enforced in the data loader/query layer, not just in the UI
Public layout includes navigation derived from site-settings global (if it exists)
scope_boundaries: Do NOT redefine CMS collections or globals in this story — reuse the content model already established
Do NOT implement comments or user interaction on public pages
Do NOT implement search on public pages — that is a separate story
Do NOT implement pagination in this story — show latest items only

validation.manual_check: Public pages render published content from CMS data loaders; draft content returns 404; homepage pulls from globals when configured.

stories.title: Setup monitoring and logging
stories.description: Integrate structured logging, health checks, and error tracking across the application.
acceptance_criteria: Structured JSON logging is configured for all backend services
Log levels (debug, info, warn, error) are used consistently
Health check endpoint exists and returns service status
Unhandled errors are caught and logged with stack traces
Database connection health is included in the health check response
Request logging includes response time for public-facing routes
scope_boundaries: Do NOT integrate external monitoring services (Datadog, Sentry, etc.) — use structured logs only
Do NOT implement custom dashboards — logs should be queryable via standard tools
Do NOT add performance profiling — focus on operational visibility

validation.manual_check: Health check endpoint returns 200 with service status; logs appear in structured JSON format

stories.title: Implement backups
stories.description: Automate postgres database backups with retention policies and documented restore procedure.
acceptance_criteria: Automated postgres backup script exists and runs successfully
Backup files are stored in a configurable location
Backup retention policy is documented (e.g., keep last 7 daily backups)
Restore procedure is documented and has been tested manually
Backup uses pg_dump with custom format for efficient storage and selective restore
Backup script can run inside or outside the Docker environment
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement cloud-specific backup solutions (RDS snapshots, etc.) — use portable scripts
Do NOT implement real-time replication — periodic backups are sufficient for MVP
Do NOT implement backup monitoring or alerting — that can be added later

validation.manual_check: Backup file is created; restore script successfully restores a test postgres dump

stories.title: Add rate limiting to auth endpoints
stories.description: Protect authentication endpoints against brute-force attacks with configurable rate limiting.
acceptance_criteria: Auth endpoints return 429 Too Many Requests after exceeding the request threshold
Rate limiter uses a sliding window or token bucket algorithm with configurable limits
Responses include rate limit headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and Retry-After
Rate limiting applies to Payload endpoints /api/users/login and /api/users/create
Rate limiting is implemented as Next.js middleware or API route wrapper
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement IP-based blocking or ban lists
Do NOT implement CAPTCHA or challenge-response mechanisms
Do NOT implement distributed rate limiting — in-memory is sufficient for MVP

validation.manual_check: Rapidly hitting /api/auth/login returns 429 after threshold is exceeded

stories.title: Enforce password policy
stories.description: Enforce minimum password length of 8 characters on both client and server.
acceptance_criteria: Registration endpoint rejects passwords shorter than 8 characters with a 400 response and descriptive error message
Login form validates minimum password length of 8 characters on the client before submission
Password validation logic is centralized in a shared utility importable by both client and server
scope_boundaries: Do NOT implement password complexity rules (uppercase, special characters) beyond minimum length
Do NOT implement a password strength meter
Do NOT implement password history or reuse prevention

validation.manual_check: Registering with a 3-character password returns 400; 8+ character password succeeds

performance: Public pages should render quickly under normal load.
API responses should target low median latency for public traffic.
Public assets should be cacheable and efficiently delivered.
scalability: System should support horizontal scaling of application servers.
Database must support concurrent authenticated users.
security: All authentication flows must enforce secure password hashing.
All external APIs must require authentication or signed requests.
Role and permission boundaries must be enforced consistently.
operational: Structured logging should be used for all backend services.
Application must run in containerized environments.

philosophy: Use consistent spacing scale and typography hierarchy.
Minimize cognitive load across the application experience.

tokens.tailwind_mapping: Use Tailwind utility classes directly. Only use custom CSS for complex animations or layouts not expressible with utilities.

components.name: ContentCard
components.purpose: Card for displaying content items in lists

components.name: HeroBlock
components.purpose: Full-width hero section for landing pages

components.name: Footer
components.purpose: Site-wide footer with links and metadata

patterns: Provide clear status indicators for important workflow states.
Use progressive disclosure for advanced options.
recommendations: Use Tailwind CSS for styling — map tokens to Tailwind config or utility classes directly.
Maintain a shared component library in src/components/ui/.
Document component usage with prop types and examples.
Follow WAI-ARIA patterns for interactive components (modals, dropdowns, tabs).

risks.title: Traffic spikes
risks.impact: The application may become unavailable or slow during peak traffic.
risks.mitigation: Use caching, CDN delivery, and capacity planning for anonymous traffic.

risks.title: Content schema evolution
risks.risk: Content models may evolve after launch.
risks.impact: Schema changes can create migration and compatibility issues.
risks.mitigation: Introduce schema versioning and a migration strategy.

risks.title: Media storage growth
risks.risk: Media assets can grow quickly over time.
risks.impact: Local storage may become insufficient or costly to manage.
risks.mitigation: Use object storage with lifecycle and retention rules.

directories.purpose: Next.js app router — route groups and pages
Base UI primitives (Button, Input, Modal, etc.)
Shared utilities, helpers, and business logic
Payload Media collection with upload config

entities.name: User
entities.description: System user with authentication credentials and role assignment.

entities.name: Article
entities.description: Editorial content item managed through a draft-to-publish workflow.

entities.name: Category
entities.description: Taxonomy for organizing and filtering content.

entities.name: MediaAsset
entities.description: Uploaded image, document, or file managed in the media library.

business_rules: Only users with reviewer or admin role can approve content for publication.
Published content cannot be edited directly — create a new draft version instead.
Deleting published content requires admin role.
Scheduled publishing requires content to be in approved/in_review state.
The scheduler checks for due content on a configurable interval and publishes automatically.
Media assets must pass file type and size validation before storage.
